Two codes are proposed: - Code A (Fixed): `00, 01, 10, 11` (length = 2 for all) - Code B (Huffman): `0, 10, 110, 111` Compute (i) the Entropy $H$, (ii) the average code length for both codes, and (iii) the compression ratio $C$ and relative data redundancy $R_D$ for Code B relative to Code A. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the three types of image redundancies with one real-world example for each. Describe how Huffman coding eliminates coding redundancy. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Step-by-Step** **Step 1 — Compute Entropy $H$:** $$ H = - \left[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 \right] $$ $$ H = - \left[ 0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.25 \cdot (-2) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) \right] $$ $$ H = - \left[ -0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375 \right] = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Step 2 — Average Length for Code A (Fixed):** $$ \bar{L}_A = (2)(0.5) + (2)(0.25) + (2)(0.125) + (2)(0.125) = 2.00 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Step 3 — Average Length for Code B (Huffman):** $$ \bar{L}_B = (1)(0.5) + (2)(0.25) + (3)(0.125) + (3)(0.125) $$ $$ \bar{L}_B = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Step 4 — Compression Ratio and Redundancy:** $$ C = \frac{n_A}{n_B} = \frac{2.00}{1.75} = 1.143 $$ $$ R_D = 1 - \frac{1}{C} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.143} = 1 - 0.875 = 0.125 \text{ (or } 12.5\% \text{)} $$ **Efficiency of Code B:** $\eta_B = H / \bar{L}_B = 1.75 / 1.75 = 1.0$ (100% — optimal Huffman code). --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Theory** 1. **Coding Redundancy** — Fixed-length codes use the same number of bits regardless of symbol frequency. **Example:** A fax machine using 12 bits per pixel when most pixels are white. **Elimination:** Variable-length codes (Huffman). 2. **Interpixel Redundancy** — Adjacent pixels are highly correlated. **Example:** Satellite images of uniform crop fields. **Elimination:** DPCM, transform coding. 3. **Psychovisual Redundancy** — HVS cannot detect subtle intensity/color changes. **Example:** JPEG chroma subsampling (4:2:0). **Elimination:** Quantization. **Huffman Coding Eliminates Coding Redundancy** by: - Assigning short codes to high-probability symbols and long codes to low-probability ones. - Satisfying the **prefix property** (no codeword is a prefix of another) — ensuring unique decodability. - Achieving $\bar{L} \to H$ (entropy bound) for any source distribution. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the general model of an image compression system. Identify and justify which block is responsible for removing (i) coding redundancy, (ii) interpixel redundancy, and (iii) psychovisual redundancy. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A $3 \times 3$ image uses 3 gray levels with probabilities $p_r(r_0) = 0.6$, $p_r(r_1) = 0.3$, $p_r(r_2) = 0.1$. The code assignment is $l(r_0) = 1$, $l(r_1) = 2$, $l(r_2) = 3$. Calculate the average code length, entropy, coding efficiency, and total bits for the image. If the image is recompressed with an optimal Huffman code, what is the theoretical minimum average code length? **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Step-by-Step** **Step 1 — Average Code Length:** $$ \bar{L} = (1)(0.6) + (2)(0.3) + (3)(0.1) = 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.3 = 1.5 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Step 2 — Entropy:** $$ H = - \left[ 0.6 \log_2 0.6 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1 \right] $$ $$ H = - \left[ 0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) + 0.3 \cdot (-1.7370) + 0.1 \cdot (-3.3219) \right] $$ $$ H = - \left[ -0.4422 - 0.5211 - 0.3322 \right] = 1.2955 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Step 3 — Coding Efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{H}{\bar{L}} = \frac{1.2955}{1.5} = 0.8637 \text{ (or } 86.37\% \text{)} $$ **Step 4 — Total Bits for the Image ($3 \times 3 = 9$ pixels):** $$ n_1 = 9 \cdot 1.5 = 13.5 \approx 14 \text{ bits (rounded up)} $$ **Step 5 — Theoretical Minimum (Huffman Optimality):** Since Huffman coding is optimal, $\bar{L}_{\min} = H = 1.2955$ bits/symbol, and the minimum total bits $= 9 \cdot 1.2955 \approx 11.66 \approx 12$ bits. **Compression ratio (vs. fixed 8-bit baseline):** $C = \dfrac{9 \cdot 8}{14} = \dfrac{72}{14} \approx 5.14$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2-3 Marks Each):** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply** $M \times N$ — many students compute $\bar{L}$ but forget the total bit count. Always end with $n_1 = M \cdot N \cdot \bar{L}$. > 2. **Unit confusion on $\log_2$** — never use $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$. KTU strictly expects $\log_2$ for entropy in bits. > 3. **Huffman optimality misuse** — Huffman is optimal for symbol-by-symbol (scalar) coding, but **NOT** always optimal for block coding. Mention this nuance only if asked. > 4. **Confusing $R_D = 1 - 1/C$ with $R_D = 1 - \eta$** — both formulas are valid, but they apply in different contexts. Use the one that matches the given data. > 5. **Rounding errors** — KTU accepts up to 3 decimal places. Always show intermediate steps. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Redundancy (Module 4)** - **Three types:** Coding, Interpixel, Psychovisual. - **Coding Redundancy** is removed by variable-length codes (Huffman, Arithmetic, Shannon-Fano). - **Interpixel Redundancy** is removed by prediction (DPCM) and transformation (DCT, Wavelets). - **Psychovisual Redundancy** is removed by quantization — and is the **only** lossy step. - **Key formulas (in order of computation):** $\bar{L} \to H \to \eta \to R_D$. - **Huffman Optimality Theorem:** $\bar{L}_{\min} = H$ (entropy bound, achievable). - **Compression Ratio:** $C = n_1 / n_2 \ge 1$. - **Relative Redundancy:** $R_D = 1 - 1/C = 1 - \eta$, with $0 \le R_D \le 1$. - **Efficiency Range:** $0 < \eta \le 1$; $\eta = 1$ means perfectly optimal coding. - **Total Bits Formula:** $n_1 = M \cdot N \cdot \bar{L}$ (variable) or $n_1 = M \cdot N \cdot k$ (fixed). - **Autocorrelation $\gamma \to 1$** indicates maximum interpixel redundancy. - **Psychovisual redundancy is irreversible** — once quantized, original data is lost. - **Always use $\log_2$** in entropy calculations for KTU problems. - **Common exam trap:** A code with $\bar{L} < H$ is impossible (violates Shannon's theorem). - **Memory aid:** "**C**oding uses **C**odes, **I**nterpixel uses **I**nformation between pixels, **P**sychovisual uses **P**erception."

topics.title: Classification of Image Compression Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT865 - IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Image Compression Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEICT865 (Image Processing)**, **image compression** is the art and science of reducing the number of bits required to represent an image while preserving the essential information it carries. The **classification of image compression schemes** refers to the systematic taxonomy of techniques used to achieve this reduction, grouped primarily by whether the original image data can be perfectly reconstructed from the compressed data, and by the underlying redundancy-removal mechanism exploited.

Formally, an image compression scheme is classified into two primary families:
1. **Lossless (Information-Preserving) Compression** — exact bit-for-bit reconstruction is guaranteed.
2. **Lossy (Information-Losing) Compression** — reconstruction is approximate, trading fidelity for much higher compression.

These are further sub-classified based on the redundancy type (coding, spatial, or psychovisual) being exploited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to *classify, differentiate, and apply* lossless (Huffman, Arithmetic, LZW, Run-Length) and lossy (Transform coding via DCT/Wavelet, Predictive coding, Vector Quantization) schemes. Examiner frequently asks **2-mark/3-mark comparison tables** between lossless and lossy techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Suitcase Packer"**
>
> Imagine you are packing for a one-week vacation:
>
> - **Lossless compression** = packing every shirt *exactly* as it was (no wrinkles, no folding changes). You can unpack and re-create the original pile perfectly, but you only save a little space (≈ 2:1 to 3:1 ratio). This is how medical scans, satellite raw data, and legal documents are compressed — **no pixel can be wrong**.
>
> - **Lossy compression** = rolling shirts into vacuum bags and discarding socks you "won't need". When you unpack, the shirts look *almost* the same, and you saved a *lot* of space (≈ 10:1 to 100:1 ratio). This is how JPEGs and streaming videos work — **the human eye forgives tiny errors**.
>
> The **classification** is simply deciding *which kind of traveler you are*: a museum curator (lossless) or a backpacker (lossy).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The classification of schemes is judged against three standard performance metrics (must be memorized verbatim for KTU exams):

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio** | $C_R$ | 1 : 1 to 100 : 1 |
| **Bits Per Pixel** | $bpp$ | 0.1 – 8.0 |
| **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $PSNR$ | **30 dB – 50 dB** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification Tree

Every image compression scheme under Module 4 fits into one of these branches:

- **Lossless Compression**
  - *Coding Redundancy Removal* → Huffman Coding, Arithmetic Coding
  - *Spatial Redundancy Removal* → Run-Length Encoding (RLE), Lempel-Ziv-Welch (LZW)
  - *Hybrid Lossless* → PNG pipeline (Filter + Dictionary + Huffman)
- **Lossy Compression**
  - *Predictive Coding* → Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM), Delta Modulation
  - *Transform Coding* → Discrete Cosine Transform (JPEG), Discrete Wavelet Transform (JPEG 2000)
  - *Block-Based Quantization* → Vector Quantization (VQ)
- **Hybrid / Practical Standards** → JPEG (DCT + Huffman + RLE), JPEG 2000 (DWT + Arithmetic + ROI)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Why classify?** Different images (medical vs. satellite vs. selfies) have different tolerance for error. Classification helps the engineer *choose* the right tool for the right job.

**How does each family work?**

1. **Lossless (Entropy Coding)**
   - **Why**: Removes *coding redundancy* — assigns shorter codes to more probable symbols.
   - **How**: Builds a probability model of pixel intensities → generates variable-length prefix codes.
   - **KTU favourite**: Huffman Tree construction & average length $L_{avg}$ calculation.

2. **Lossless (Spatial / Dictionary Coding)**
   - **Why**: Removes *spatial redundancy* — neighboring pixels are often identical (sky, walls).
   - **How**: Encodes *runs* of identical symbols (RLE) or replaces repeated patterns with dictionary references (LZW).

3. **Lossy (Predictive Coding — DPCM)**
   - **Why**: Removes *spatial redundancy* between adjacent pixels via prediction.
   - **How**: Predicts pixel $f(n)$ from past pixels; transmits only the *residual* error $e(n) = f(n) - \hat{f}(n)$, then quantizes and encodes.

4. **Lossy (Transform Coding — DCT/DWT)**
   - **Why**: Energy compaction — most image energy packs into few coefficients.
   - **How**: Transform → Quantize (lossy step) → Entropy encode. The transform itself is *reversible*; quantization is what causes loss.

5. **Vector Quantization (VQ)**
   - **Why**: Exploits *correlations between blocks* of pixels.
   - **How**: Maps $k$-dimensional pixel blocks to nearest entry in a *codebook* of representative blocks.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** Memorize the *definitions*, *formulas*, and *typical value ranges* of these equations. Numerical problems in Part B almost always use one of them.

$$
C_R = \frac{n_1}{n_2} \quad \text{(Compression Ratio)}
$$

$$
bpp = \frac{\text{Total bits after compression}}{\text{Total number of pixels}}
$$

$$
MSE = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\left[f(i,j) - f'(i,j)\right]^{2}
$$

$$
PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{(L-1)^{2}}{MSE}\right)\; \text{dB}
$$

$$
H = -\sum_{k=0}^{L-1} p(r_k)\,\log_{2}\,p(r_k) \quad \text{(Shannon Entropy, bits/pixel)}
$$

$$
L_{avg} = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} l(r_k)\,p(r_k) \quad \text{(Average Code Length)}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{H}{L_{avg}} \quad \text{(Coding Efficiency)}
$$

$$
R_{d} = 1 - \eta \quad \text{(Relative Redundancy)}
$$

### Safe Markdown Table (no broken pipes)

| Parameter | Lossless Scheme | Lossy Scheme |
|---|---|---|
| Reconstruction | Bit-exact | Approximate |
| Typical CR | 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 | 10 : 1 to 100 : 1 |
| Typical PSNR | Infinity (no error) | 30 dB to 45 dB |
| Best Use Case | Medical, archival | Web, streaming |
| Exploits Redundancy | Coding / Spatial | Psychovisual |
| Example Standard | PNG, GIF | JPEG, JPEG 2000 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (DICOM lossless)** → Zero-error diagnostics; CR $\approx$ 2.5 : 1.
- **Earth Observation (JPEG 2000 lossless for raw bands)** → Preserves spectral fidelity.
- **Streaming & CCTV (JPEG / H.264 intra-frames)** → 50 : 1 compression with imperceptible loss.
- **Deep Space Imaging (NASA ICER)** → Hybrid wavelet + progressive transmission.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Mandate:** Numerical problems on entropy, average code length, compression ratio, and PSNR appear every semester. The derivations below are the *exact* working steps a topper is expected to show.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compression Ratio (CR) and Bits Per Pixel (bpp)

**Problem Setup:** An image of size $256 \times 256$ pixels, 8 bits/pixel, is compressed into 49,152 bytes. Find $C_R$ and $bpp$.

**Step 1 — Compute original size:**
$$
n_1 = 256 \times 256 \times 8 \; \text{bits} = 524{,}288 \; \text{bits} = 65{,}536 \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify compressed size:**
$$
n_2 = 49{,}152 \; \text{bytes} = 49{,}152 \times 8 \; \text{bits} = 393{,}216 \; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CR formula:**
$$
C_R = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{65{,}536}{49{,}152}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio (divide both by 49,152):**
$$
C_R = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33 : 1
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $bpp$:**
$$
bpp = \frac{\text{Compressed bits}}{\text{Total pixels}} = \frac{393{,}216}{256 \times 256} = \frac{393{,}216}{65{,}536} = 6.0 \; \text{bpp}
$$

**Conclusion:** The image was compressed from 8 bpp to 6 bpp, achieving a 1.33 : 1 compression ratio.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Entropy $H$ (Classification Justification)

**Problem Setup:** A 4-symbol alphabet $r = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4\}$ has probabilities $p = \{0.5,\, 0.25,\, 0.125,\, 0.125\}$. Compute entropy $H$.

**Step 1 — Apply entropy formula term-by-term:**
$$
H = -\sum_{k=1}^{4} p(r_k)\,\log_{2}\,p(r_k)
$$

**Step 2 — Expand summation:**
$$
H = -\left[ p(r_1)\log_{2}p(r_1) + p(r_2)\log_{2}p(r_2) + p(r_3)\log_{2}p(r_3) + p(r_4)\log_{2}p(r_4) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
H = -\left[ 0.5\log_{2}(0.5) + 0.25\log_{2}(0.25) + 0.125\log_{2}(0.125) + 0.125\log_{2}(0.125) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each logarithm:**
$$
\log_{2}(0.5) = -1,\quad \log_{2}(0.25) = -2,\quad \log_{2}(0.125) = -3
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through:**
$$
H = -\left[ (0.5)(-1) + (0.25)(-2) + (0.125)(-3) + (0.125)(-3) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the bracket:**
$$
H = -\left[ -0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375 \right] = -\left[ -1.75 \right] = 1.75 \; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

**Conclusion:** The entropy of the source is **1.75 bits/symbol**, the theoretical lower bound for lossless coding. Any lossless scheme achieving $L_{avg} = 1.75$ is perfectly optimal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Code Length, Efficiency & Redundancy

**Problem Setup:** Using Huffman coding on the same alphabet, the assigned codeword lengths are $l = \{1, 2, 3, 3\}$ symbols.

**Step 1 — Compute $L_{avg}$:**
$$
L_{avg} = \sum_{k=1}^{4} l(r_k)\,p(r_k) = (1)(0.5) + (2)(0.25) + (3)(0.125) + (3)(0.125)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**
$$
L_{avg} = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute coding efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \frac{H}{L_{avg}} = \frac{1.75}{1.75} = 1.0 \; (\text{or } 100\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute relative redundancy:**
$$
R_d = 1 - \eta = 1 - 1.0 = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\eta = 1$, the Huffman code is *optimal* — it achieves the entropy bound, confirming that this lossless scheme is fully classified as **entropy-optimal**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PSNR from MSE (Lossy Performance Metric)

**Problem Setup:** A $512 \times 512$, 8-bit grayscale image is reconstructed with $MSE = 65$. Compute $PSNR$.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard formula:**
$$
PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(L-1)^{2}}{MSE}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $L - 1 = 255$ for 8-bit image:**
$$
PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(255)^{2}}{65}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$
(255)^{2} = 65{,}025
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio:**
$$
\frac{65{,}025}{65} \approx 1000.38
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm:**
$$
\log_{10}(1000.38) \approx 3.0002
$$

**Step 6 — Final PSNR:**
$$
PSNR = 10 \times 3.0002 \approx 30.0 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** A reconstructed image with $MSE = 65$ yields $PSNR \approx 30$ dB — a **barely acceptable** lossy quality for general-purpose use.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use What?)

| Image Type | Lossless or Lossy? | Recommended Scheme | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chest X-Ray | Lossless | DPCM + Huffman | Diagnostic accuracy mandatory |
| Satellite multispectral | Near-Lossless | JPEG-LS / JPEG 2000 | Spectral fidelity critical |
| CCTV surveillance footage | Lossy | DCT + Huffman (JPEG) | Storage $\gg$ pixel-perfect |
| Newspaper archive scan | Lossless | LZW (TIFF) | Archival permanence |
| WhatsApp photo | Lossy | DCT + Quantization (JPEG) | Bandwidth and human-eye tolerance |
| Fingerprint biometric | Lossless | Wavelet + Arithmetic | Match accuracy depends on minutiae |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Flowchart

The diagram below maps the complete classification hierarchy as required by KTU Module 4, along with the typical application zones. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `n2`) to ensure safe Mermaid compilation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["IMAGE COMPRESSION SCHEMES"] --> n2["LOSSLESS COMPRESSION"]
    n1 --> n3["LOSSY COMPRESSION"]
    n1 --> n4["HYBRID STANDARDS"]

    n2 --> n2a["CODING REDUNDANCY"]
    n2 --> n2b["SPATIAL REDUNDANCY"]
    n2 --> n2c["HYBRID LOSSLESS"]

    n2a --> n2a1["HUFFMAN CODING"]
    n2a --> n2a2["ARITHMETIC CODING"]
    n2b --> n2b1["RUN LENGTH ENCODING"]
    n2b --> n2b2["LZW DICTIONARY"]
    n2c --> n2c1["PNG PIPELINE"]

    n3 --> n3a["PREDICTIVE CODING"]
    n3 --> n3b["TRANSFORM CODING"]
    n3 --> n3c["VECTOR QUANTIZATION"]

    n3a --> n3a1["DPCM"]
    n3a --> n3a2["DELTA MODULATION"]
    n3b --> n3b1["DCT BASED JPEG"]
    n3b --> n3b2["DWT BASED JPEG2000"]
    n3c --> n3c1["BLOCK VQ"]
    n3c --> n3c2["LBG ALGORITHM"]

    n4 --> n4a["JPEG"]
    n4 --> n4b["JPEG 2000"]
    n4 --> n4c["WEBP"]

    n2a1 -.uses.-> m1["ENTROPY H"]
    n2b1 -.uses.-> m2["RUN LENGTH METRICS"]
    n3b1 -.uses.-> m3["PSNR AND MSE"]
    n3b2 -.uses.-> m3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Generic Compression Pipeline

This block-level functional architecture shows how every scheme — regardless of being classified as lossless or lossy — fits into a unified encoder–decoder pipeline. Use this for drawing questions (2-mark/5-mark) in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["ORIGINAL IMAGE F OF M N"] --> map["PIXEL MAPPING OR TRANSFORM"]
    map --> qtz["QUANTIZER LOSSY STEP OPTIONAL"]
    qtz --> sym["SYMBOL ENCODER HUFFMAN OR ARITHMETIC"]
    sym --> bit["COMPRESSED BIT STREAM"]

    bit --> dec["SYMBOL DECODER"]
    dec --> iqtz["DEQUANTIZER"]
    iqtz --> imap["INVERSE MAPPING OR TRANSFORM"]
    imap --> out["RECONSTRUCTED IMAGE F DASH"]

    map -.if lossless.-> sym
    qtz -.omitted for lossless.-> x["SKIPPED"]
```

**Interpretation:** The classification of a scheme is dictated by *which blocks are active*.
- If **Quantizer** is omitted → **Lossless** classification.
- If **Quantizer** is active and **Dequantizer** cannot exactly invert → **Lossy** classification.
- If both operate (e.g., JPEG) → **Hybrid Lossy**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### **Q1. Classify the major image compression schemes. Give one example for each class.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Image compression schemes are broadly classified into:
1. **Lossless Compression** — preserves exact original data after reconstruction. Example: Huffman Coding, Arithmetic Coding, LZW.
2. **Lossy Compression** — achieves higher compression by accepting small, often imperceptible distortion. Example: JPEG (DCT-based), Vector Quantization, JPEG 2000 (DWT-based).
3. **Hybrid / Near-Lossless** — combines entropy coding with controlled quantization, e.g., JPEG, JPEG 2000, WebP.

Lossless schemes are used where fidelity is critical (medical, archival), while lossy schemes dominate consumer applications where CR of 10 : 1 to 100 : 1 is desired.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between coding redundancy, spatial redundancy, and psychovisual redundancy.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Coding Redundancy** — arises when symbols (pixel values) are encoded with more bits than necessary. Removed by entropy coding (Huffman, Arithmetic).
- **Spatial Redundancy** — arises from strong correlation between neighboring pixels. Removed by predictive coding, RLE, LZW, and transform coding.
- **Psychovisual (Irrelevant) Redundancy** — information ignored by the human visual system (e.g., fine high-frequency details). Removed by quantization in lossy schemes.

These three redundancies form the **basis of classification** of every image compression technique.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

### **Q A. (a)** Explain in detail the classification of image compression schemes with suitable block diagrams. Compare lossless and lossy techniques on the basis of compression ratio, reconstruction quality, and complexity. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, CO4]`

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Image compression reduces the number of bits required to represent an image by exploiting three fundamental redundancies: coding, spatial, and psychovisual. Schemes are classified into lossless and lossy categories.

**Classification (3 marks):**

*Lossless Compression* — Bit-exact reconstruction guaranteed.
- Entropy Coding: Huffman, Arithmetic, Shannon-Fano
- Dictionary/LZ Coding: LZW, LZ77
- Run-Length Coding
- Predictive Lossless: Lossless DPCM

*Lossy Compression* — Approximate reconstruction; achieves far higher compression.
- Predictive Lossy: DPCM + Quantizer, Delta Modulation
- Transform Coding: DCT (JPEG), DWT (JPEG 2000)
- Block Truncation Coding (BTC)
- Vector Quantization (VQ, LBG)

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Lossless | Lossy |
|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio | 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 | 10 : 1 to 100 : 1 |
| Reconstruction Quality | Exact (PSNR = $\infty$) | Approximate (30–45 dB) |
| Computational Complexity | Low to Moderate | Moderate to High |
| Application | Medical, archival | Web, streaming |
| Redundancy Exploited | Coding & Spatial | Psychovisual |

**Block Diagram Reference (1 mark):** Refer to *Section 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram* — lossless schemes omit the quantizer; lossy schemes retain it.

---

### **Q A. (b)** A $512 \times 512$, 8-bit grayscale image has the following symbol probability distribution: $p(r_1) = 0.4$, $p(r_2) = 0.3$, $p(r_3) = 0.2$, $p(r_4) = 0.1$. Construct the Huffman code, compute the average code length $L_{avg}$, coding efficiency $\eta$, and relative redundancy $R_d$. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, CO4, Apply Level]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort probabilities in descending order (1 mark):**
$$
p(r_1)=0.4,\;\; p(r_2)=0.3,\;\; p(r_3)=0.2,\;\; p(r_4)=0.1
$$

**Step 2 — Huffman Tree Construction (2 marks):**
- Combine $0.2 + 0.1 = 0.3$ → node A
- Combine $0.3 + 0.3 = 0.6$ → node B
- Combine $0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0$ → root

Assigning binary digits (0 to top, 1 to bottom):

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $l(r_k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $r_1$ | 0.4 | 0 | 1 |
| $r_2$ | 0.3 | 10 | 2 |
| $r_3$ | 0.2 | 110 | 3 |
| $r_4$ | 0.1 | 111 | 3 |

**Step 3 — Compute Entropy $H$ (1.5 marks):**
$$
H = -\sum p(r_k)\log_2 p(r_k)
$$

$$
H = -\left[0.4\log_2(0.4) + 0.3\log_2(0.3) + 0.2\log_2(0.2) + 0.1\log_2(0.1)\right]
$$

$$
H = -\left[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.3(-1.7370) + 0.2(-2.3219) + 0.1(-3.3219)\right]
$$

$$
H = -\left[-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322\right] = 1.8465 \; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Average Code Length (1 mark):**
$$
L_{avg} = (1)(0.4) + (2)(0.3) + (3)(0.2) + (3)(0.1)
$$

$$
L_{avg} = 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.3 = 1.9 \; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 5 — Coding Efficiency (0.75 mark):**
$$
\eta = \frac{H}{L_{avg}} = \frac{1.8465}{1.9} = 0.9718 \;\; (97.18\%)
$$

**Step 6 — Relative Redundancy (0.75 mark):**
$$
R_d = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9718 = 0.0282 \; (2.82\%)
$$

**Conclusion:** The Huffman code is highly efficient (97.18%), confirming it is a **near-optimal lossless classification** under entropy coding.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

### **Q B. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the general image compression model. How does the presence or absence of the quantizer classify a scheme as lossy or lossless? **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, CO4, Understand Level]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The general image compression model consists of two halves — Encoder and Decoder.

**Encoder:**
1. *Mapper* — performs transformation (e.g., DCT, DWT) or pixel mapping to decorrelate data.
2. *Quantizer* — reduces the precision of mapper output (lossy step).
3. *Symbol Encoder* — produces compressed bitstream using entropy coding.

**Decoder:**
1. *Symbol Decoder* — recovers quantized symbols.
2. *Inverse Mapper* — reconstructs image from mapped coefficients.

**Role of Quantizer in Classification (3 marks):**
- **Quantizer Present** → The mapping from input to output is *many-to-one*. Hence reconstruction cannot be exact. **Scheme is LOSSY**.
- **Quantizer Absent** → Every block is invertible; output is bit-identical to input. **Scheme is LOSSLESS**.
- **Quantizer Bypassed / Identity** → Trivially lossless.

This single decision — whether the quantizer block is included — is the **definitive criterion** for classifying any scheme.

**Example Mapping (1 mark):**
- Huffman Coding → Mapper + Symbol Encoder (no quantizer) → **Lossless**
- JPEG → DCT Mapper + Quantizer + Huffman Encoder → **Lossy**
- Lossless JPEG → DCT Mapper + Huffman (no quantizer) → **Lossless**

---

### **Q B. (b)** A $256 \times 256$ image is compressed to 32,768 bytes. The reconstructed image has $MSE = 49$ when compared to the original. Calculate the compression ratio, the bits per pixel after compression, and the PSNR of the reconstruction. Comment on the quality. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, CO4, Apply Level]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original Size (1 mark):**
$$
n_1 = 256 \times 256 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \; \text{bits} = 65{,}536 \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed Size (1 mark):**
$$
n_2 = 32{,}768 \; \text{bytes} = 262{,}144 \; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio (1.5 marks):**
$$
C_R = \frac{65{,}536}{32{,}768} = 2 : 1
$$

**Step 4 — Bits Per Pixel (1.5 marks):**
$$
bpp = \frac{262{,}144}{256 \times 256} = \frac{262{,}144}{65{,}536} = 4.0 \; \text{bpp}
$$

**Step 5 — PSNR (1.5 marks):**
For 8-bit image, $L - 1 = 255$.
$$
PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(255)^{2}}{49}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1326.28)
$$

$$
PSNR = 10 \times 3.1226 \approx 31.23 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Quality Comment (0.5 mark):**
A PSNR of **31.23 dB** indicates *acceptable* lossy reconstruction. Visible artifacts may be present but the image remains intelligible for general-purpose use.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L_{avg}$ with $H$** — $H$ is the *lower bound*; $L_{avg}$ is the *actual* average code length from the Huffman tree. Swapping them inverts $\eta$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(L-1)^2$ term** — For 8-bit images, the numerator is $255^2$, NOT $256^2$. PSNR errors of $\pm 0.05$ dB creep in from this slip.
> 3. **Wrong unit in CR** — $C_R$ is *dimensionless*. Always express it as `x : 1` (e.g., `2 : 1`).
> 4. **Mixing up $bpp$ (compressed) vs original bpp** — KTU asks compressed $bpp$. Divide compressed bits by total pixels.
> 5. **Huffman tie-breaking** — When probabilities tie, any valid Huffman tree is accepted; but you MUST draw the tree step-by-step, not just write codewords.
> 6. **Classification mistake** — JPEG 2000 with lossless mode is lossless; with lossy mode it is lossy. Always mention the *mode* before classifying.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classification Axis** is the **Quantizer block**: present → lossy, absent → lossless.
- **Three Redundancies**: Coding, Spatial, Psychovisual — every scheme exploits at least one.
- **Lossless Family**: Huffman, Arithmetic, Shannon-Fano, LZW, RLE, PNG pipeline.
- **Lossy Family**: DPCM, DCT (JPEG), DWT (JPEG 2000), VQ, BTC, Fractal.
- **Hybrid Standards**: JPEG, JPEG 2000, WebP, HEIF.
- **Entropy $H$** = theoretical minimum bits/symbol; **$L_{avg}$** = actual bits/symbol.
- **Efficiency $\eta = H / L_{avg}$**, **Redundancy $R_d = 1 - \eta$**.
- **Compression Ratio $C_R = n_1 / n_2$** (dimensionless ratio, typically `x : 1`).
- **$bpp = \text{Compressed bits} / \text{Total pixels}$**.
- **$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}((L-1)^2 / MSE)$ in dB**; 30 dB acceptable, 40 dB excellent.
- **Typical Use Rule**: Medical/Archival → Lossless; Web/Streaming → Lossy; Satellite raw → Lossless/Near-lossless.
- **For KTU numericals**: always show the Huffman tree, all $\log_2$ evaluations, and final numerical answers boxed.
- **CO Mapping**: This topic aligns with **CO4** — *Apply appropriate compression techniques for various image types and evaluate performance metrics.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Huffman Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT865 - IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Huffman Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Huffman Coding** is a **statistical, lossless, variable-length entropy encoding technique** developed by **David A. Huffman (1952)**. It assigns shorter binary codewords to symbols (e.g., image gray levels) that occur more frequently, and longer codewords to symbols that occur less frequently, thereby minimizing the **average code length** $L_{avg}$ and approaching the theoretical lower bound given by the source **entropy** $H(S)$.

In the context of **Digital Image Processing**, Huffman coding is the final entropy-coding stage of several classical compression pipelines, most notably the **JPEG baseline standard** and **JPEG 2000 (via MQ-coder)**, where it encodes the quantized DCT coefficients and category/run-length pairs into a compact bitstream.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam Answer Sheet" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (The "Smart Student" Model):**
> Imagine a teacher distributing a 1000-word English essay to be graded. Instead of grading every letter, the teacher notices:
> - The letter **'E'** appears **120 times**.
> - The letter **'Z'** appears only **1 time**.
>
> A **fixed-length code** would assign every letter an **8-bit** ASCII word (e.g., 'E' → `01000101`, 'Z' → `01011010`). This wastes bits on rare letters.
>
> Huffman coding acts like a **smart shorthand notebook**: it tells the teacher — "Just write 'E' as the symbol `01` (2 bits) and write 'Z' as `01111110` (8 bits)." Frequently used letters get **short signatures**, and rare letters get **long signatures**.
> The decoder (the teacher's grading key) can uniquely distinguish every shorthand because **no code is a prefix of another** (the **Prefix Property**).

## 1.3 Why Huffman Coding Matters in Image Processing

| Application Area | Role of Huffman Coding |
|---|---|
| **JPEG Compression** | Encodes quantized DCT coefficients & DC category values. |
| **Facsimile (Fax) Transmission** | Used in **Group 3 / Group 4** fax standards (Modified Huffman, MH). |
| **Medical Imaging (DICOM)** | Often combined with JPEG-LS or lossless JPEG. |
| **Archival Storage** | Compressing RAW bitmaps, satellite imagery. |
| **Entropy Lower Bound** | Reference benchmark against which any practical code is measured. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics Introduced in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **Average Code Length ($L_{avg}$)** — measured in **bits per symbol**.
> - **Entropy ($H(S)$)** — measured in **bits per symbol**.
> - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** — dimensionless ratio.
> - **Redundancy ($R$)** — dimensionless ratio.
> - **Compression Ratio ($CR$)** — dimensionless ratio.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Foundation — The Three Pillars

The Huffman algorithm rests on three foundational principles. Understanding **why** each exists is critical for the 14-mark KTU questions.

### Pillar 1: The Prefix Condition (Uniquely Decodable Codes)
> A code is said to satisfy the **prefix condition** if **no codeword is a prefix of any other codeword** in the code set.
>
> This guarantees that the bitstream can be decoded **unambiguously without delimiters** between codewords — the decoder simply walks a binary tree until it hits a leaf.

### Pillar 2: Variable-Length Coding Principle
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Shannon-Fano-Huffman Theorem (informal):** In any information source, frequently occurring symbols should be encoded with **fewer bits**, and rarely occurring symbols with **more bits**. This is the only way to minimize $L_{avg}$ subject to the prefix constraint.

### Pillar 3: Optimality
> Huffman coding produces an **optimal prefix code** — no other prefix code can yield a smaller $L_{avg}$ for the same probability distribution. This was proven by Huffman's greedy construction (1952).

## 2.2 The Huffman Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **List** all source symbols $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n$ with their probabilities $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$.
2. **Sort** the symbols in **descending order of probability** (largest first).
3. **Combine** the **two symbols with the lowest probabilities** into a new composite node whose probability is the **sum** of the two.
4. **Re-insert** the composite node back into the list and **re-sort** in descending order.
5. **Repeat** Steps 3 and 4 until only **one composite node remains** (the **root**, with total probability = 1).
6. **Trace the tree** from the root to each leaf (symbol). Assign **'0'** to the left branch and **'1'** to the right branch (convention may vary).
7. **Read off the codeword** for each symbol by concatenating the branch labels along the path.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are directly tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers (PEICT865 Module 4).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Self-Information (Information Content)** | $I(s_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ | **bits/symbol** |
| 2 | **Entropy of Source** | $H(S) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(s_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | **bits/symbol** |
| 3 | **Average Code Length** | $L_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot l_i$ | **bits/symbol**; $l_i$ = length of codeword for $s_i$ |
| 4 | **Coding Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{H(S)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\%$ | **% (dimensionless)** |
| 5 | **Coding Redundancy** | $R = 1 - \eta = \dfrac{L_{avg} - H(S)}{L_{avg}}$ | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | **Kraft-McMillan Inequality** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | Necessary for any prefix code |
| 7 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}$ | $n_1$ = bits in original, $n_2$ = bits in compressed |
| 8 | **Compression Factor** | $CF = \dfrac{CR}{1}$ or $1/CR$ (definition variant) | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Saving Percentage** | $S\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right) \times 100\%$ | **%** |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **JPEG**: Huffman tables are stored in the image header so the decoder can rebuild the same codebook.
- **Satellite Imaging**: NOAA / ISRO ground stations rely on Huffman-equivalent entropy coding to downlink multi-gigapixel earth-observation swaths within bandwidth budgets.
- **MP3 / AAC Audio**: Although MP3 uses **Huffman** and AAC uses **Arithmetic coding**, the entropy-coding principle is identical — frequently occurring spectral coefficients get shorter codes.
- **5G & Beyond**: Source-channel coding in modern wireless uses entropy coding as the final step before channel coding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 The Canonical KTU Examination Problem

> **[Exemplar KTU-Style Problem Statement]**
> An 8-bit grayscale image contains **6 gray levels** $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6$ with the following probabilities of occurrence:
>
> | Symbol | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ | $s_6$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Probability | **0.40** | **0.20** | **0.15** | **0.10** | **0.10** | **0.05** |
>
> **(a)** Construct the **Huffman code** for this source.
> **(b)** Compute the **entropy** $H(S)$, **average code length** $L_{avg}$, **coding efficiency** $\eta$, and **redundancy** $R$.
> **(c)** If the image has $N = 10{,}000$ pixels, find the **compression ratio** and the **saving percentage** versus an **8-bit fixed-length code**.

---

## 3.2 Part (a): Huffman Tree Construction — Exhaustive Step Trace

### Step 0 — Initial Probability List (sorted descending)

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &: p = 0.40 \\
s_2 &: p = 0.20 \\
s_3 &: p = 0.15 \\
s_4 &: p = 0.10 \\
s_5 &: p = 0.10 \\
s_6 &: p = 0.05 \quad \leftarrow \text{(two lowest)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Combine $s_5$ and $s_6$ (probabilities 0.10 + 0.05)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic:** Take the two lowest probabilities, add them to form a parent node, then re-sort the list.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New node } N_1 &: p = 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.15 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated list (descending):
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &: 0.40,\ s_2 : 0.20,\ N_1 : 0.15,\ s_3 : 0.15,\ s_4 : 0.10 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Combine $s_4$ and $N_1$ (probabilities 0.10 + 0.15)

> Conversion logic: The two smallest entries are now $s_4 = 0.10$ and $N_1 = 0.15$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New node } N_2 &: p = 0.10 + 0.15 = 0.25 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated list:
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &: 0.40,\ N_2 : 0.25,\ s_2 : 0.20,\ s_3 : 0.15 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine $s_3$ and $s_2$ (probabilities 0.15 + 0.20)

> Conversion logic: The two smallest now are $s_3 = 0.15$ and $s_2 = 0.20$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New node } N_3 &: p = 0.15 + 0.20 = 0.35 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated list:
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &: 0.40,\ N_3 : 0.35,\ N_2 : 0.25 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine $N_3$ and $N_2$ (probabilities 0.35 + 0.25)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New node } N_4 &: p = 0.35 + 0.25 = 0.60 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated list:
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &: 0.40,\ N_4 : 0.60 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Combine $s_1$ and $N_4$ → **Root** $R$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &: p = 0.40 + 0.60 = 1.00 \ \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Assign Branch Labels and Read Off Codewords

Using convention: **Left child = '0'**, **Right child = '1'**.

| Symbol | Path from Root | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | Root → Left | `0` | **1** |
| $s_2$ | Root → Right → Right → Right | `111` | **3** |
| $s_3$ | Root → Right → Right → Left | `110` | **3** |
| $s_4$ | Root → Right → Left → Right | `101` | **3** |
| $s_5$ | Root → Right → Left → Left → Right | `1001` | **4** |
| $s_6$ | Root → Right → Left → Left → Left | `1000` | **4** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verify the Prefix Property:** No codeword is a prefix of another.
> - `0` is not a prefix of `111`, `110`, `101`, `1001`, `1000` ✓
> - `111` is not a prefix of `110`, `101`, `1001`, `1000` ✓
> - `110` is not a prefix of `111`, `101`, `1001`, `1000` ✓
> - `101` is not a prefix of `1001`, `1000` ✓
> - `1001` is not a prefix of `1000` ✓
>
> **All prefix conditions satisfied → Uniquely decodable.** ✓

---

## 3.3 Part (b): Entropy, Average Length, Efficiency, Redundancy

### 3.3.1 Entropy $H(S)$

Using $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\bigl[ 0.40 \log_2(0.40) + 0.20 \log_2(0.20) + 0.15 \log_2(0.15) \\
&\qquad + 0.10 \log_2(0.10) + 0.10 \log_2(0.10) + 0.05 \log_2(0.05) \bigr] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
-0.40 \log_2(0.40) &= -0.40 \times (-1.3219) &= 0.5288 \\
-0.20 \log_2(0.20) &= -0.20 \times (-2.3219) &= 0.4644 \\
-0.15 \log_2(0.15) &= -0.15 \times (-2.7370) &= 0.4105 \\
-0.10 \log_2(0.10) &= -0.10 \times (-3.3219) &= 0.3322 \\
-0.10 \log_2(0.10) &= -0.10 \times (-3.3219) &= 0.3322 \\
-0.05 \log_2(0.05) &= -0.05 \times (-4.3219) &= 0.2161 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:
$$
H(S) = 0.5288 + 0.4644 + 0.4105 + 0.3322 + 0.3322 + 0.2161 = 2.2842 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

### 3.3.2 Average Code Length $L_{avg}$

Using $L_{avg} = \sum p_i \cdot l_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= (0.40)(1) + (0.20)(3) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(3) + (0.10)(4) + (0.05)(4) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.20 \\
&= 2.35 \text{ bits/symbol} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Coding Efficiency $\eta$ and Redundancy $R$

$$
\eta = \frac{H(S)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.2842}{2.35} \times 100\% = 97.20\%
$$

$$
R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9720 = 0.0280 = 2.80\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Noiseless Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem):**
> The theorem guarantees: $H(S) \leq L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$.
> We have $2.2842 \leq 2.35 < 3.2842$. ✓ **Theorem satisfied.**

---

## 3.4 Part (c): Compression Ratio and Saving Percentage

For $N = 10{,}000$ pixels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= N \times 8 = 10{,}000 \times 8 = 80{,}000 \text{ bits (fixed-length)} \\
n_2 &= N \times L_{avg} = 10{,}000 \times 2.35 = 23{,}500 \text{ bits (Huffman)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Compression Ratio:
$$
CR = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{80{,}000}{23{,}500} \approx 3.40 : 1
$$

Saving Percentage:
$$
S\% = \left(1 - \frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{23{,}500}{80{,}000}\right) \times 100\% = 70.625\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Interpretation:** The Huffman code reduces the storage requirement from **80,000 bits to 23,500 bits**, saving approximately **70.63%** of the original bandwidth/storage while remaining **completely lossless**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Huffman Binary Tree (Computed Tree for Section 3 Example)

Below is the fully constructed Huffman binary tree. Branch labels follow the standard convention **'0' = Left, '1' = Right**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Root (P = 1.00)"]
    N4["N4 (P = 0.60)"]
    N3["N3 (P = 0.35)"]
    N2["N2 (P = 0.25)"]
    N1["N1 (P = 0.15)"]
    s1["s1 (P = 0.40) - Code 0"]
    s2["s2 (P = 0.20) - Code 111"]
    s3["s3 (P = 0.15) - Code 110"]
    s4["s4 (P = 0.10) - Code 101"]
    s5["s5 (P = 0.10) - Code 1001"]
    s6["s6 (P = 0.05) - Code 1000"]

    R ---|"0"| s1
    R ---|"1"| N4
    N4 ---|"0"| N3
    N4 ---|"1"| N2
    N3 ---|"0"| s2
    N3 ---|"1"| s3
    N2 ---|"0"| N1
    N2 ---|"1"| s4
    N1 ---|"0"| s6
    N1 ---|"1"| s5
```

## 4.2 Huffman Algorithm — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Symbol-Probability List"] --> B["Step 1: Sort List Descending by Probability"]
    B --> C{"Step 2: More than 1 Node?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Step 3: Pick 2 Lowest-Probability Nodes"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Merge into Parent Node (Sum Probabilities)"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Re-insert Parent and Re-sort"]
    F --> C
    C -- "No" --> G["Step 6: Single Root Node Remains"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Assign 0 to Left, 1 to Right Branches"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Read Codewords from Root to Each Leaf"]
    I --> J["Output: Optimal Prefix Code Table"]
```

## 4.3 Encoder vs Decoder Symmetry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENC["ENCODER"]
        IMG1["Source Image Pixels"] --> HUFF["Huffman Code Table"]
        HUFF --> BIT["Compressed Bitstream"]
    end
    subgraph DEC["DECODER"]
        BIT2["Compressed Bitstream"] --> DEC_TREE["Huffman Decode Tree"]
        DEC_TREE --> IMG2["Reconstructed Image (Lossless)"]
    end
    BIT --> BIT2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Architectural Insight:** Both encoder and decoder must use the **identical code tree**. In JPEG, the standard Huffman tables are predefined and stored in the image header. For adaptive Huffman (e.g., fax Group 4), the encoder updates the tree and the decoder tracks changes — both stay synchronized.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define Huffman coding. List any two of its salient features.**

**Model Answer:**
Huffman coding is a **statistical, lossless, variable-length encoding technique** that assigns shorter binary codewords to more frequently occurring source symbols and longer codewords to less frequent ones, producing an **optimal prefix code** that minimizes the **average code length** $L_{avg}$.

**Salient features:**
1. It produces **uniquely decodable codes** because the **prefix condition** is satisfied (no codeword is the prefix of any other).
2. It yields **optimal** compression — no other prefix code can produce a smaller $L_{avg}$ for the same probability distribution.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**What is the significance of the Kraft-McMillan inequality in the design of prefix codes?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Kraft-McMillan inequality**, given by $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$, is a **necessary and sufficient condition** for the existence of a **prefix code** with codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \dots, l_n$. Before constructing any prefix code, we must verify this inequality; otherwise, the code is not realizable. It guarantees that the assigned code lengths can form a **complete binary tree** without overlap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Step-mark deduction:** Examiners allocate marks for **each merge step** of the Huffman tree. Skipping intermediate lists costs up to **3 marks**.
> - **Branch-label convention:** The convention (0 = left, 1 = right) is **not universal**. If you don't state your convention, you may lose **1 mark** if your codewords are inverted from the examiner's.
> - **Log base:** Always use **$\log_2$** for information-theoretic quantities. Using natural log (ln) loses **1 mark**.
> - **Entropy vs Length:** A common error is computing $H(S)$ using the lengths $l_i$ instead of probabilities $p_i$. Always use $-p_i \log_2 p_i$.
> - **Efficiency formula:** Many students write $\eta = L_{avg}/H(S)$ — this is **inverted** and leads to $> 100\%$, losing **2 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU ESE July 2024 Pattern]

A digital image contains **5 gray levels** with probabilities:
$s_1 = 0.30,\ s_2 = 0.25,\ s_3 = 0.20,\ s_4 = 0.15,\ s_5 = 0.10$.

**(a)** Construct the **Huffman code tree** and determine the codeword for each symbol. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the **entropy**, **average code length**, **efficiency**, and **redundancy** of the code. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### Part (a) — Huffman Tree Construction

**Step 1:** Sort probabilities descending: $0.30,\ 0.25,\ 0.20,\ 0.15,\ 0.10$.

**Step 2:** Combine two lowest: $0.15 + 0.10 = 0.25$ → **Node $N_1$**.

Updated list: $0.30,\ 0.25,\ 0.25,\ 0.20$.

**Step 3:** Combine two lowest: $0.20 + 0.25 = 0.45$ → **Node $N_2$** (using $s_3$ and one of the $0.25$s — say $N_1$).

Updated list: $0.45,\ 0.30,\ 0.25$.

**Step 4:** Combine two lowest: $0.25 + 0.30 = 0.55$ → **Node $N_3$**.

Updated list: $0.55,\ 0.45$.

**Step 5:** Combine: $0.55 + 0.45 = 1.00$ → **Root**.

**Codeword Table (Convention: 0 = Left, 1 = Right):**

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.30 | `00` | 2 |
| $s_2$ | 0.25 | `01` | 2 |
| $s_3$ | 0.20 | `10` | 2 |
| $s_4$ | 0.15 | `110` | 3 |
| $s_5$ | 0.10 | `111` | 3 |

### Part (b) — Performance Metrics

**Entropy:**
$$
H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -(0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 \\
&\quad + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10) \\
&= -(0.30 \times (-1.737) + 0.25 \times (-2.000) + 0.20 \times (-2.322) \\
&\quad + 0.15 \times (-2.737) + 0.10 \times (-3.322)) \\
&= 0.5211 + 0.5000 + 0.4644 + 0.4105 + 0.3322 \\
H(S) &= 2.2282 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average Code Length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= (0.30)(2) + (0.25)(2) + (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(3) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.30 \\
L_{avg} &= 2.25 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \frac{2.2282}{2.25} \times 100\% = 99.03\%
$$

**Redundancy:**
$$
R = 1 - 0.9903 = 0.0097 = 0.97\%
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU ESE Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

A monochrome image contains **7 gray levels** with the following probability distribution:

| $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ | $s_6$ | $s_7$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | 0.18 | 0.15 | 0.13 | 0.12 | 0.12 | 0.10 |

**(a)** Build the **Huffman code** and tabulate the codewords. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Determine $H(S)$, $L_{avg}$, $\eta$, and the **compression ratio** compared with a **3-bit fixed-length code**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### Part (a) — Huffman Tree Construction

**Initial sorted list:**
$0.20,\ 0.18,\ 0.15,\ 0.13,\ 0.12,\ 0.12,\ 0.10$

**Merge Step 1:** Combine $0.10$ and $0.12$ → $N_1 = 0.22$
Updated: $0.22,\ 0.20,\ 0.18,\ 0.15,\ 0.13,\ 0.12$

**Merge Step 2:** Combine $0.12$ and $0.13$ → $N_2 = 0.25$
Updated: $0.25,\ 0.22,\ 0.20,\ 0.18,\ 0.15$

**Merge Step 3:** Combine $0.15$ and $0.18$ → $N_3 = 0.33$
Updated: $0.33,\ 0.25,\ 0.22,\ 0.20$

**Merge Step 4:** Combine $0.20$ and $0.22$ → $N_4 = 0.42$
Updated: $0.42,\ 0.33,\ 0.25$

**Merge Step 5:** Combine $0.25$ and $0.33$ → $N_5 = 0.58$
Updated: $0.58,\ 0.42$

**Merge Step 6:** Combine $0.42$ and $0.58$ → **Root** $= 1.00$

**Final Code Table (0 = Left, 1 = Right):**

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.20 | `01` | 2 |
| $s_2$ | 0.18 | `00` | 2 |
| $s_3$ | 0.15 | `111` | 3 |
| $s_4$ | 0.13 | `110` | 3 |
| $s_5$ | 0.12 | `101` | 3 |
| $s_6$ | 0.12 | `1001` | 4 |
| $s_7$ | 0.10 | `1000` | 4 |

### Part (b) — Metrics

**Entropy:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\bigl[ 0.20\log_2 0.20 + 0.18\log_2 0.18 + 0.15\log_2 0.15 \\
&\quad + 0.13\log_2 0.13 + 0.12\log_2 0.12 + 0.12\log_2 0.12 + 0.10\log_2 0.10 \bigr] \\
&= 0.4644 + 0.4452 + 0.4105 + 0.3826 + 0.3671 + 0.3671 + 0.3322 \\
H(S) &= 2.7691 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average Code Length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= (0.20)(2) + (0.18)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.13)(3) \\
&\quad + (0.12)(3) + (0.12)(4) + (0.10)(4) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.36 + 0.45 + 0.39 + 0.36 + 0.48 + 0.40 \\
L_{avg} &= 2.84 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \frac{2.7691}{2.84} \times 100\% = 97.50\%
$$

**Compression Ratio (vs 3-bit fixed-length):**
$$
CR = \frac{3}{2.84} \approx 1.056 : 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Note that with only 7 symbols ($\lceil\log_2 7\rceil = 3$ bits fixed), the room for compression is small. Huffman shines brightest with images having **highly skewed probability distributions** (e.g., medical X-rays where most pixels are background).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This for Last-Minute Review):**

- [x] **Huffman Coding** = lossless, variable-length, optimal **prefix** code.
- [x] **Goal:** Minimize $L_{avg} = \sum p_i l_i$.
- [x] **Algorithm:** Repeatedly merge the **two lowest-probability** nodes.
- [x] **Branch Convention:** State explicitly — usually **0 = Left, 1 = Right**.
- [x] **Prefix Property:** No codeword is a prefix of any other → **uniquely decodable**.
- [x] **Entropy Formula:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ **(bits/symbol)**.
- [x] **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{H(S)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\%$ — must be **$\leq 100\%$**.
- [x] **Redundancy:** $R = 1 - \eta$.
- [x] **Shannon's Noiseless Coding Theorem:** $H(S) \leq L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$.
- [x] **Kraft-McMillan Inequality:** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.
- [x] **JPEG Usage:** Huffman is the final entropy coder after DCT + Quantization.
- [x] **Adaptive Huffman:** Used in fax (Group 3/4) — tree updates dynamically.
- [x] **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to **re-sort** after each merge.
- [x] **Common Pitfall:** Using natural log instead of $\log_2$.
- [x] **Limitation:** Huffman is **suboptimal** when symbol probabilities are not powers of $\frac{1}{2}$ (Arithmetic coding approaches the entropy bound more tightly).
- [x] **Exam Tip:** Always draw the tree, write the merge steps as a numbered list, and **box the final $L_{avg}$ and $\eta$**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT865 - IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arithmetic Coding** is a **lossless data compression** technique defined under the KTU 2024 Image Processing syllabus (Module 4: Image Compression). It encodes an entire sequence of source symbols into a **single fractional number (a tag)** lying within the interval **[0, 1)**. Unlike symbol-by-symbol coding, it removes the restriction of allocating an integer number of bits per symbol, thereby approaching the **entropy limit** of the source.

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Arithmetic Coding** maps a string of source symbols $S = s_1 s_2 \dots s_n$ drawn from an alphabet with known probability distribution $P(s_i)$ to a real number $T \in [0, 1)$ such that any value chosen from the **final sub-interval** of length equal to the joint probability of the sequence uniquely represents it. The average code length approaches $H(S)$, the **Shannon entropy** of the source.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Dividing a Ruler" Analogy

Imagine a unit-length **ruler** stretching from **0 to 1**. The ruler is divided into segments proportional to the probabilities of each symbol. To encode a sequence, you start at the full ruler and then **repeatedly zoom into a sub-segment** determined by the next symbol, narrowing the available space each time.

- If a symbol is **highly probable**, it occupies a large segment → small narrowing.
- If a symbol is **rare**, it occupies a small segment → sharp narrowing.

After processing the entire string, the surviving sub-interval is so tiny that a single number chosen from inside it is sufficient to encode the **whole message**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of guessing a number between 0 and 1. A friend says "between 0 and 0.5?", then "between 0.25 and 0.375?", and so on. Each question halves (or proportionally reduces) the search interval. The final tiny interval is the message.

## 1.3 Why Arithmetic Coding? — Comparison Hook

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Huffman Coding** → assigns integer-bit codes; efficiency drops when symbol probabilities are not powers of 1/2.
> - **Arithmetic Coding** → fractional bits; achieves compression ratio very close to the theoretical **entropy bound**.

## 1.4 Key Physical / Mathematical Metrics

- **Entropy Bound (Shannon):** $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i$ (in **bits/symbol**)
- **Average Code Length:** Approaches $H(S)$ in arithmetic coding.
- **Interval Width (Range):** Always initialized to **1.0** and monotonically decreases.
- **Number of Distinct Symbols (N):** Defines the **dictionary size** of the coder.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Recursive Interval Narrowing

The arithmetic coder works on **cumulative probability ranges** rather than raw probabilities. Let the alphabet be $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_N\}$ with probabilities $p_i$ and cumulative distribution:

$$F(s) = \sum_{i: a_i < s} p_i$$

The range is maintained as $[\text{low}, \text{high})$, initialized to $[0, 1)$. On reading symbol $s_k$:

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Compute the current **range width** $R = \text{high} - \text{low}$.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **lower bound** of the new range: $\text{low}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + R \cdot F(s_k)$.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **upper bound** of the new range: $\text{high}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + R \cdot \left(F(s_k) + p_{s_k}\right)$.
- **Step 4:** Replace $[\text{low}, \text{high})$ with $[\text{low}_{\text{new}}, \text{high}_{\text{new}})$.
- **Step 5:** Repeat for each symbol. After $n$ symbols, output **any number $T$** that lies in the final interval.

The width of the final interval equals the **joint probability** $P(S) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} p_{s_k}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** The probability of every possible message is proportional to the area (length) of its assigned interval. This is what makes arithmetic coding optimal — common sequences get long intervals, requiring fewer bits to specify.

## 2.2 The "How" Behind the Decoder

The decoder receives the tag $T$ and reconstructs the sequence. It maintains the same low/high interval and, for each step:

- Computes the **normalized position** of $T$ within the current interval: $\text{value} = (T - \text{low}) / (\text{high} - \text{low})$.
- Identifies which symbol's cumulative range contains $\text{value}$.
- Outputs that symbol and updates the interval the same way the encoder did.

## 2.3 Practical Issues (Important for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

> [!WARNING]
> **Finite-Precision Problem:** Real hardware cannot store infinite-precision real numbers. Two classical solutions exist:
> 1. **Integer Arithmetic Coding:** Scale the interval to fixed-size **integers** (e.g., 32-bit). Output bits are emitted when the high and low values share leading bits (a technique used in **JBIG** and **JPEG 2000**).
> 2. **Carry-Over / Renormalization:** Whenever the interval no longer straddles 0.5, the common leading bit is transmitted and the interval is doubled.

- **Underflow:** Interval becomes too small to represent.
- **Overflow:** Interval values grow beyond machine precision.
- **Termination:** A special **end-of-stream (EOS)** symbol is appended to the message so the decoder knows when to stop.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Source Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits per symbol |
| Interval Width (Range) | $R_k = \text{high}_k - \text{low}_k$ | dimensionless, starts at 1.0 |
| New Lower Bound | $\text{low}_{k+1} = \text{low}_k + R_k \cdot F(s_{k+1})$ | computed per symbol |
| New Upper Bound | $\text{high}_{k+1} = \text{low}_k + R_k \cdot \left(F(s_{k+1}) + p_{s_{k+1}}\right)$ | strictly greater than $\text{low}_{k+1}$ |
| Tag Constraint | $T \in [\text{low}_n, \text{high}_n)$ | $T$ is a real number in [0, 1) |
| Final Interval Width | $\text{high}_n - \text{low}_n = \prod_{k=1}^{n} p_{s_k}$ | equals joint probability |
| Decoder Normalized Value | $\text{value} = (T - \text{low}_k) / R_k$ | used to identify next symbol |
| Average Code Length | $L_{\text{avg}} \geq H(S)$ | arithmetic coding achieves equality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Arithmetic coding is the backbone of modern image and document compression — **JBIG** (bi-level images), **JPEG 2000**, **H.264/HEVC CABAC**, **PNG**, and **PDF** all rely on arithmetic coding (or close variants) because it yields 5–10 % better compression than Huffman on typical imagery.

## 2.5 Applications in Image Processing

- **Bi-level (fax) image compression** — JBIG, JBIG2.
- **Continuous-tone image compression** — JPEG 2000 (Context-Adaptive QM Coder).
- **Video coding** — CABAC in H.264 / H.265.
- **Document archival** — DjVu, PDF.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Encoder + Decoder)

Consider an alphabet $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4\}$ with probabilities:

$$p_1 = 0.2, \quad p_2 = 0.2, \quad p_3 = 0.4, \quad p_4 = 0.2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Verify: $0.2 + 0.2 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0$ ✓ (valid probability mass function).

**Cumulative distribution ranges:**

| Symbol $s$ | Probability $p_s$ | Range $[F(s), F(s)+p_s)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $a_1$ | 0.2 | $[0.0, 0.2)$ |
| $a_2$ | 0.2 | $[0.2, 0.4)$ |
| $a_3$ | 0.4 | $[0.4, 0.8)$ |
| $a_4$ | 0.2 | $[0.8, 1.0)$ |

### 3.1.1 Encoding the Sequence $S = a_1\, a_2\, a_3\, a_4$

**Initialisation.** $\text{low}_0 = 0.0$, $\text{high}_0 = 1.0$, range $R_0 = 1.0$.

---

**Step 1 — Process $a_1$ (range $[0.0, 0.2)$):**

$$R_0 = 1.0 - 0.0 = 1.0$$

$$\text{low}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.0 = 0.0000$$

$$\text{high}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.2 = 0.2000$$

So the interval becomes $[0.0000, 0.2000)$.

---

**Step 2 — Process $a_2$ (range $[0.2, 0.4)$):**

$$R_1 = 0.2000 - 0.0000 = 0.2000$$

$$\text{low}_2 = 0.0000 + 0.2000 \times 0.2 = 0.0400$$

$$\text{high}_2 = 0.0000 + 0.2000 \times 0.4 = 0.0800$$

So the interval becomes $[0.0400, 0.0800)$.

---

**Step 3 — Process $a_3$ (range $[0.4, 0.8)$):**

$$R_2 = 0.0800 - 0.0400 = 0.0400$$

$$\text{low}_3 = 0.0400 + 0.0400 \times 0.4 = 0.0560$$

$$\text{high}_3 = 0.0400 + 0.0400 \times 0.8 = 0.0720$$

So the interval becomes $[0.0560, 0.0720)$.

---

**Step 4 — Process $a_4$ (range $[0.8, 1.0)$):**

$$R_3 = 0.0720 - 0.0560 = 0.0160$$

$$\text{low}_4 = 0.0560 + 0.0160 \times 0.8 = 0.0688$$

$$\text{high}_4 = 0.0560 + 0.0160 \times 1.0 = 0.0720$$

So the **final interval** is $[0.0688, 0.0720)$.

**Verification of final width (should equal joint probability):**

$$P(a_1 a_2 a_3 a_4) = 0.2 \times 0.2 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.0032$$

$$\text{high}_4 - \text{low}_4 = 0.0720 - 0.0688 = 0.0032 \;\checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Choose the tag** as the midpoint: $T = (0.0688 + 0.0720)/2 = 0.0704$. Any value in $[0.0688, 0.0720)$ is a valid representation. In practice, the **shortest binary fraction** inside the interval is transmitted.

---

### 3.1.2 Decoding the Tag $T = 0.0704$

**Initialisation.** $\text{low}_0 = 0.0$, $\text{high}_0 = 1.0$.

**Iteration 1.** Compute normalized value:

$$\text{value} = \frac{T - \text{low}_0}{\text{high}_0 - \text{low}_0} = \frac{0.0704 - 0.0}{1.0 - 0.0} = 0.0704$$

Since $0.0704 \in [0.0, 0.2)$, the decoded symbol is $\mathbf{a_1}$. Update $\text{low} = 0.0$, $\text{high} = 0.2$.

**Iteration 2.**

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.0704 - 0.0}{0.2 - 0.0} = 0.3520$$

Since $0.3520 \in [0.2, 0.4)$, the decoded symbol is $\mathbf{a_2}$. Update $\text{low} = 0.04$, $\text{high} = 0.08$.

**Iteration 3.**

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.0704 - 0.04}{0.08 - 0.04} = \frac{0.0304}{0.0400} = 0.7600$$

Since $0.7600 \in [0.4, 0.8)$, the decoded symbol is $\mathbf{a_3}$. Update $\text{low} = 0.056$, $\text{high} = 0.072$.

**Iteration 4.**

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.0704 - 0.056}{0.072 - 0.056} = \frac{0.0144}{0.0160} = 0.9000$$

Since $0.9000 \in [0.8, 1.0)$, the decoded symbol is $\mathbf{a_4}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoded sequence:** $a_1\, a_2\, a_3\, a_4$ — exactly the original message. Decoding is lossless.

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Table

| Iteration $k$ | Input Symbol | New Low | New High | Range Width |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | — | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 |
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0.0000 | 0.2000 | 0.2000 |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0.0400 | 0.0800 | 0.0400 |
| 3 | $a_3$ | 0.0560 | 0.0720 | 0.0160 |
| 4 | $a_4$ | 0.0688 | 0.0720 | 0.0032 |

The final width $0.0032$ equals the joint probability of the sequence, confirming correctness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Arithmetic Encoder — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START ENCODER] --> B[INITIALISE LOW 0 AND HIGH 1]
    B --> C[READ SYMBOL S FROM INPUT STREAM]
    C --> D[COMPUTE RANGE EQUALS HIGH MINUS LOW]
    D --> E[COMPUTE NEW HIGH EQUALS LOW PLUS RANGE TIMES UPPER CUMULATIVE OF S]
    E --> F[COMPUTE NEW LOW EQUALS LOW PLUS RANGE TIMES LOWER CUMULATIVE OF S]
    F --> G{ANY MORE SYMBOLS LEFT}
    G -->|YES| C
    G -->|NO| H[SELECT TAG T ANYWHERE IN FINAL INTERVAL]
    H --> I[EMIT BINARY REPRESENTATION OF T]
    I --> J[END]
```

## 4.2 Arithmetic Decoder — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    K[START DECODER] --> L[INITIALISE LOW 0 AND HIGH 1]
    L --> M[READ INPUT TAG T]
    M --> N[COMPUTE RANGE EQUALS HIGH MINUS LOW]
    N --> O[COMPUTE VALUE EQUALS TAG MINUS LOW DIVIDED BY RANGE]
    O --> P[FIND SYMBOL WHOSE CUMULATIVE RANGE CONTAINS VALUE]
    P --> Q[OUTPUT IDENTIFIED SYMBOL]
    Q --> R[UPDATE LOW AND HIGH USING SYMBOL CUMULATIVE]
    R --> S{END OF MESSAGE MARKER REACHED}
    S -->|NO| N
    S -->|YES| T[END]
```

## 4.3 Interval Narrowing — Conceptual Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[INITIAL UNIT INTERVAL 0 TO 1] --> A1[AFTER SYMBOL A1 SUBNARROW 0 TO 0.2]
    A1 --> A2[AFTER SYMBOL A2 SUBNARROW 0.04 TO 0.08]
    A2 --> A3[AFTER SYMBOL A3 SUBNARROW 0.056 TO 0.072]
    A3 --> A4[AFTER SYMBOL A4 FINAL TAG WINDOW 0.0688 TO 0.072]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The diagrams above mirror the worked numerical example in Section 3. Each box represents the *current* encoder/decoder interval, illustrating the **monotonic shrinkage** that ultimately produces the final tag window.

## 4.4 Coder–Decoder Symmetry Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    SRC[INPUT SYMBOL SEQUENCE] --> ENC[ARITHMETIC ENCODER]
    ENC --> TAG[COMPRESSED BITSTREAM REPRESENTING TAG T]
    TAG --> DEC[ARITHMETIC DECODER]
    DEC --> OUT[RECONSTRUCTED SYMBOL SEQUENCE]
    ENC -. USES .-> PMF[SYMBOL PROBABILITY MODEL]
    DEC -. USES .-> PMF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**

### Q1. Define Arithmetic Coding. How is it different from Huffman Coding? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Arithmetic coding is a lossless entropy-coding technique that maps an entire input message into a single fractional number $T \in [0, 1)$ lying inside an interval whose length equals the message's joint probability.
- **Difference (1.5 marks):** Huffman coding assigns an **integer number of bits** to each symbol independently, making it suboptimal when probabilities are not negative powers of 0.5. Arithmetic coding treats the **whole message as one unit**, achieving average code lengths arbitrarily close to the **source entropy** $H(S)$.

---

### Q2. Why must an end-of-stream (EOS) symbol be appended to the message before arithmetic coding? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
- The decoder does not know a priori **how many symbols** were encoded. (1 mark)
- Without an EOS marker, the decoder cannot decide **when to stop** producing symbols — it could continue forever because any value in the final interval is valid for arbitrarily long messages. (1 mark)
- Appending a known EOS symbol (whose probability is set low, e.g., $10^{-6}$) makes the final interval narrow to a known termination point, signalling the end. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End-Semester Pattern, Module 4]**

### ❓ Question A — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step algorithm** for arithmetic coding with a neat block diagram. State the formulas for updating the *low* and *high* bounds. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A source emits symbols from alphabet $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with probabilities
$p(a) = 0.4$, $p(b) = 0.3$, $p(c) = 0.2$, $p(d) = 0.1$.
Encode the sequence $S = a\, b\, c\, d$ using arithmetic coding. Show all iterations and choose an appropriate tag. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — Algorithm & Formulas

1. **Input:** Sequence of symbols $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n$ and a probability model $\{p_i\}$.
2. **Initialise:** $\text{low} = 0$, $\text{high} = 1$.
3. **For each symbol $s_k$:**
    - Compute range $R = \text{high} - \text{low}$.
    - Compute cumulative lower bound $F(s_k)$.
    - **Update formulas (2 marks):**

$$\text{high}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + R \cdot \left(F(s_k) + p_{s_k}\right)$$

$$\text{low}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + R \cdot F(s_k)$$

4. **After processing all symbols**, pick a tag $T$ anywhere in the final interval $[\text{low}_n, \text{high}_n)$ and output its binary representation.
5. **(Block diagram)** — refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the encoder flow.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — Worked Example

**Cumulative ranges:**

| Symbol | Probability | Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $a$ | 0.4 | $[0.0, 0.4)$ |
| $b$ | 0.3 | $[0.4, 0.7)$ |
| $c$ | 0.2 | $[0.7, 0.9)$ |
| $d$ | 0.1 | $[0.9, 1.0)$ |

**Step 1 — Symbol $a$ (range $[0.0, 0.4)$):**

$$R_0 = 1.0 - 0.0 = 1.0$$

$$\text{low}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.0 = 0.0000$$

$$\text{high}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.4 = 0.4000$$

Interval: $[0.0000, 0.4000)$

**Step 2 — Symbol $b$ (range $[0.4, 0.7)$):**

$$R_1 = 0.4000 - 0.0000 = 0.4000$$

$$\text{low}_2 = 0.0 + 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.1600$$

$$\text{high}_2 = 0.0 + 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.2800$$

Interval: $[0.1600, 0.2800)$

**Step 3 — Symbol $c$ (range $[0.7, 0.9)$):**

$$R_2 = 0.2800 - 0.1600 = 0.1200$$

$$\text{low}_3 = 0.1600 + 0.1200 \times 0.7 = 0.2440$$

$$\text{high}_3 = 0.1600 + 0.1200 \times 0.9 = 0.2680$$

Interval: $[0.2440, 0.2680)$

**Step 4 — Symbol $d$ (range $[0.9, 1.0)$):**

$$R_3 = 0.2680 - 0.2440 = 0.0240$$

$$\text{low}_4 = 0.2440 + 0.0240 \times 0.9 = 0.2656$$

$$\text{high}_4 = 0.2440 + 0.0240 \times 1.0 = 0.2680$$

**Final interval:** $[0.2656, 0.2680)$

**Verification:**

$$P(a\,b\,c\,d) = 0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.0024$$

$$\text{high}_4 - \text{low}_4 = 0.2680 - 0.2656 = 0.0024 \;\checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Chosen tag:** $T = (0.2656 + 0.2680)/2 = 0.2668$. The encoder transmits the shortest binary fraction inside this window, typically $\approx 8$ to 9 bits for 4 input symbols (compared to the entropy $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i \approx 1.846$ bits/symbol, totalling 7.38 bits — a near-optimal result).

---

### ❓ Question B — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare **Arithmetic Coding** with **Huffman Coding** under the following heads: efficiency, complexity, patent issues, handling of symbol probabilities, and output format. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A compressed bitstream represents the tag $T = 0.4575$ for a 4-symbol message from the alphabet $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with probabilities
$p(a) = 0.2$, $p(b) = 0.4$, $p(c) = 0.1$, $p(d) = 0.3$.
**Decode** the message using arithmetic coding. Show every iteration. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Huffman Coding | Arithmetic Coding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output Format | Integer-bit codes per symbol | Single fractional tag for entire message |
| Efficiency | Sub-optimal if $p_i \neq 2^{-k}$ | Approaches entropy $H(S)$ arbitrarily closely |
| Computational Complexity | Low — greedy tree construction | Higher — multiply/add per symbol |
| Patent Issues | Free to use | Historically patented (IBM); core algorithms now expired / royalty-free (CABAC in H.264 licensed) |
| Probability Handling | Per-symbol assignment | Uses cumulative distribution $F(s)$ |
| Suitability for Adaptive Models | Limited | Excellent (model updated as symbols arrive) |

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Decoding

**Cumulative ranges:**

| Symbol | Probability | Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $a$ | 0.2 | $[0.0, 0.2)$ |
| $b$ | 0.4 | $[0.2, 0.6)$ |
| $c$ | 0.1 | $[0.6, 0.7)$ |
| $d$ | 0.3 | $[0.7, 1.0)$ |

**Iteration 1 —** $T = 0.4575$, range $[0.0, 1.0)$:

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.4575 - 0.0}{1.0 - 0.0} = 0.4575$$

Since $0.4575 \in [0.2, 0.6)$, decoded symbol is $\mathbf{b}$.
Update: $\text{low} = 0.2$, $\text{high} = 0.6$.

**Iteration 2 —**

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.4575 - 0.2}{0.6 - 0.2} = \frac{0.2575}{0.4} = 0.64375$$

Since $0.64375 \in [0.6, 0.7)$, decoded symbol is $\mathbf{c}$.
Update: $\text{low} = 0.2 + 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.44$, $\text{high} = 0.2 + 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.48$.

**Iteration 3 —**

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.4575 - 0.44}{0.48 - 0.44} = \frac{0.0175}{0.04} = 0.4375$$

Since $0.4375 \in [0.2, 0.6)$, decoded symbol is $\mathbf{b}$.
Update: $\text{low} = 0.44 + 0.04 \times 0.2 = 0.448$, $\text{high} = 0.44 + 0.04 \times 0.6 = 0.464$.

**Iteration 4 —**

$$\text{value} = \frac{0.4575 - 0.448}{0.464 - 0.448} = \frac{0.0095}{0.016} = 0.59375$$

Since $0.59375 \in [0.2, 0.6)$, decoded symbol is $\mathbf{b}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoded message:** $\mathbf{b\,c\,b\,b}$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps in arithmetic coding questions:**
> 1. **Off-by-one on cumulative ranges** — students often use $F(s) + p_s$ inclusive on the wrong side, collapsing adjacent intervals. Use **half-open intervals** $[F(s), F(s)+p_s)$ strictly.
> 2. **Forgetting to reset** $\text{low}/\text{high}$ **at each iteration** — they must be updated recursively, **not** recomputed from scratch.
> 3. **Confusing decoder normalization** — the value is $(T-\text{low})/(\text{high}-\text{low})$ *for the current interval*, not $(T-\text{low}_{\text{initial}})$.
> 4. **Skipping the final tag justification** — always show that the **interval width equals the joint probability** for full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the EOS symbol** in long answers — examiners award 1 mark for explicitly mentioning it.
> 6. **Not stating that the coder and decoder must share the *exact* same probability model** — otherwise the bitstream cannot be decoded.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Arithmetic coding = lossless entropy coding** that maps a **whole message** to **one fractional number** in $[0, 1)$.
- **Core update equations** (memorise!):

$$\text{high}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + R \cdot (F(s) + p_s), \quad \text{low}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + R \cdot F(s)$$

- **Initial range:** $\text{low} = 0$, $\text{high} = 1$.
- **Final interval width** $=$ **joint probability** of the message sequence.
- **Decoder** reverses the process using the normalized value $\text{value} = (T - \text{low})/R$.
- **Achieves entropy bound** — average length $\to H(S)$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Huffman vs Arithmetic:** Huffman → integer bits/symbol; Arithmetic → fractional bits, near-optimal.
- **Requires a known probability model** (cumulative distribution $F(s)$) at both encoder and decoder.
- **Practical implementations** use **integer arithmetic** with **renormalization** to avoid underflow/overflow (e.g., **QM Coder** in JPEG 2000, **CABAC** in H.264).
- **Applications:** JBIG, JBIG2, JPEG 2000, H.264/HEVC, PNG, PDF, DjVu.
- **EOS symbol** is mandatory so the decoder knows when to stop.
- **Tag selection rule:** Pick any number in the final interval — the midpoint is conventional and minimises worst-case binary length.
- **For KTU 14-mark answers**, always:
  (i) tabulate cumulative ranges,
  (ii) show every iteration explicitly,
  (iii) verify final width = joint probability,
  (iv) mention practical / real-world utility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dictionary-Based Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT865 - IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dictionary-Based Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Dictionary-Based Compression** is a class of *lossless* data compression techniques in which a dynamically constructed or pre-defined **dictionary (codebook)** of recurring source patterns (strings, byte sequences, or pixel runs) is maintained. Instead of transmitting the raw symbols, the compressor transmits **short fixed-length indices** that reference entries in this dictionary, while the decompressor rebuilds the original data using an identical copy of the dictionary.

In the context of **Image Processing (KTU PEICT865, Module 4)**, dictionary-based methods are extensively used in standards such as **GIF (Graphics Interchange Format)**, **TIFF**, and parts of **PNG**, where they exploit the strong spatial redundancy (repeated pixel intensities, run-lengths, and texture motifs) found in natural and synthetic images.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4):** The syllabus specifically expects knowledge of the **LZW (Lempel–Ziv–Welch)** algorithm, its encoder/decoder logic, dictionary initialization, code-word assignment, and the special *KwKwK* rule for handling dictionary overflow.

## 1.2 Types of Dictionary Schemes

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Static Dictionary** | Dictionary is fixed and known to both encoder and decoder in advance | Pre-loaded codebook of common pixel values |
| **Adaptive (Dynamic) Dictionary** | Dictionary is built on-the-fly during encoding, and *implicitly* reconstructed by the decoder from the encoded stream | **LZ77, LZ78, LZW** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator's Notebook" 🗒️

Imagine an English-to-Spanish translator who keeps a **notebook**. Every time a long English phrase appears (say, *"Good morning, how are you?"*), the translator writes it down in the notebook and assigns it a short number, like **#7**. From that point on, the translator simply writes **"#7"** instead of the full phrase. A partner who keeps an identical notebook (and updates it the same way) can instantly reconstruct the original phrase.

- The **notebook = the dictionary**.
- The **short number = the codeword (index)**.
- The **notebook grows** as new phrases are encountered — this is *adaptive* compression.
- Both parties **never send the dictionary itself**; it is built from the stream — this is the genius of **LZW** in image coding.

> [!NOTE]
> The brilliance of dictionary coding lies in the fact that **the dictionary is not transmitted** — it is reconstructed deterministically by the decoder from the bit stream, saving significant bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Dictionary-Based Coding

### 2.1.1 LZ77 — Sliding Window (1977)
- Maintains a **sliding window** divided into:
