# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A consists of 5 questions of 3 marks each, with **internal choice**. Below are two model short-answer questions, exactly aligned with the KTU December pattern.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(3 Marks)*

**"List any three key differences between JPEG and MPEG compression standards."**

**Model Answer:**

| # | JPEG | MPEG |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compresses a **single still image** | Compresses a **sequence of video frames** |
| 2 | Removes only **spatial redundancy** using DCT | Removes both **spatial and temporal** redundancy (DCT + motion vectors) |
| 3 | Uses only **intra-frame coding** | Uses **intra (I) + inter-frame (P, B) coding** |
| 4 | Standardized as **ISO/IEC 10918** | Standardized as **ISO/IEC 11172 (MPEG-1), 13818 (MPEG-2)** |
| 5 | Frame is independent; no reference to other frames | P/B-frames depend on I/P reference frames |

**(Any 3 differences = 3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *(3 Marks)*

**"What is the role of quantization in JPEG compression? Why is it considered the only lossy step?"**

**Model Answer:**

Quantization is the step in the JPEG pipeline where each DCT coefficient is divided by a corresponding entry in a **quantization table (Q-table)** and rounded to the nearest integer:

$$ F_Q(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{F(u,v)}{Q(u,v)}\right] $$

It is considered the **only lossy step** in baseline JPEG because:

1. All prior steps (DCT, color conversion, level shift) are **mathematically invertible** with no information loss.
2. Quantization **irreversibly discards information** by mapping a continuous range of values to a single integer.
3. Higher Q-table values produce more rounding error and more discarded detail, directly controlling the **rate–distortion tradeoff**.

**(3 marks — 2 for formula + 1 for irreversibility explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions are **14 marks** with a **module-level internal choice**. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B fully.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *JPEG Deep Dive*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **JPEG encoder pipeline** in detail. Identify the lossy step and justify. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 1024×1024, 8-bit grayscale image is compressed by JPEG to a file of 256 KB. Compute the **Compression Ratio (CR)**, **Relative Data Redundancy (R)**, and the **average bpp** of the compressed image. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

The JPEG encoder pipeline consists of **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Color Space Conversion (RGB → YCbCr):** The image is converted from RGB to YCbCr using the standard linear transformation. The Y channel carries luminance; Cb and Cr carry chrominance. This exploits the human visual system's lower sensitivity to color detail.

2. **Chroma Subsampling (4:2:0):** The Cb and Cr channels are downsampled by a factor of 2 in both directions, reducing total data by 50%.

3. **Block Partitioning:** The image is divided into non-overlapping **8×8 blocks** (64 pixels each). This is the fundamental processing unit.

4. **Level Shift:** Each pixel value is shifted by subtracting 128 to center the dynamic range around zero. This is required for the DCT's mathematical properties.

5. **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT):** A 2D DCT is applied to each 8×8 block, producing 64 frequency coefficients. The DC coefficient (top-left) represents the average intensity; AC coefficients represent spatial frequencies.

6. **Quantization:** Each coefficient is divided by the corresponding Q-table entry and rounded. **This is the ONLY lossy step.** High-frequency coefficients (bottom-right) are quantized more aggressively because the human eye is less sensitive to them.

7. **Zig-Zag Scan & RLE:** Coefficients are reordered from low to high frequency, concentrating zeros at the end. Run-length encoding then efficiently compresses runs of zeros.

8. **Huffman Entropy Coding:** DC differences and AC (run, size, amplitude) tokens are compressed using Huffman coding, producing the final bitstream.

**Justification of Lossy Step:** Quantization is irreversible — once a value is rounded, the original continuous information cannot be recovered. All other steps are mathematically invertible transforms or symbol re-orderings with no information loss.

*(7 marks: 5 for pipeline description + 2 for lossiness justification)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1: Compute original file size.**

$$ n_1 = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 8,388,608 \text{ bits} = 1,048,576 \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ KB} = 1 \text{ MB} $$

**Step 2: Compute compressed file size.**

$$ n_2 = 256 \text{ KB} = 256 \times 1024 \times 8 = 2,097,152 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 3: Compute Compression Ratio.**

$$ \text{CR} = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{8,388,608}{2,097,152} = 4.0 $$

**Step 4: Compute Relative Data Redundancy.**

$$ R = 1 - \frac{1}{\text{CR}} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75 \quad \text{or} \quad 75\% $$

**Step 5: Compute average bpp.**

$$ b = \frac{n_2}{\text{Total pixels}} = \frac{2,097,152}{1024 \times 1024} = \frac{2,097,152}{1,048,576} = 2.0 \text{ bits/pixel} $$

**Final Answer:** CR = 4.0, R = 75%, bpp = 2.0 bits/pixel.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for computations, 2 for interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *MPEG & Standards Comparison*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **three types of frames in MPEG (I, P, B)** with a diagram showing a typical **GOP = 12/3** structure. Describe how temporal redundancy is removed using **motion estimation and compensation**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **JPEG, JPEG 2000, and MPEG-2** compression standards in terms of application, technique used, target bitrate, and compression ratio. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**I-frames (Intra-coded):** Compressed using the JPEG-like DCT + quantization + entropy coding pipeline. They are **self-contained** — no reference to other frames. They serve as **random-access points** in the bitstream and as references for P and B-frames. I-frames have the **largest size** (lowest compression) but are essential for error recovery and seeking.

**P-frames (Predictive):** Encoded using **forward motion-compensated prediction** from the most recent I or P frame. The encoder divides the frame into 16×16 macroblocks and searches the reference frame for the best-matching block. The displacement is the **motion vector (MV) = (Δx, Δy)**. The **residual error** (difference between actual and predicted block) is DCT-compressed. P-frames are smaller than I-frames because residuals typically have much less energy than original pixel data.

**B-frames (Bidirectional):** Encoded using **interpolative prediction** from both the **past and future** reference frames. B-frames achieve the **highest compression** because the bidirectional interpolation yields even smaller residuals. However, they introduce **encoding delay** (the encoder must buffer future frames) and **re-ordering** (display order ≠ transmission order).

**GOP = 12/3 Structure:**

$$ \underbrace{I}_{1} \, B \, B \, \underbrace{P}_{4} \, B \, B \, \underbrace{P}_{7} \, B \, B \, \underbrace{P}_{10} \, B \, B $$

- Total 12 frames in the GOP
- 1 I-frame, 3 P-frames, 8 B-frames
- I-to-I spacing = 3 frames
- New GOP starts at frame 13

**Temporal Redundancy Removal Mechanism:**

The encoder performs **block matching** between the current macroblock and candidate blocks in a search window of the reference frame. The matching metric is typically **Mean Absolute Difference (MAD)**:

$$ \text{MAD}(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \vert f_t(i,j) - f_{t-1}(i+\Delta x, j+\Delta y) \vert $$

The (Δx, Δy) pair minimizing MAD is the **optimal motion vector**. The encoder then transmits:
- The motion vector (compressed via DPCM + entropy coding)
- The DCT-compressed residual $E = f_t - f_{t-1}^{\text{pred}}$

This combination of MV + small residual is far more compact than the original frame.

*(7 marks: 3 for I/P/B explanation, 2 for GOP diagram, 2 for motion compensation math)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Parameter | **JPEG** | **JPEG 2000** | **MPEG-2** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Joint Photographic Experts Group | JPEG 2000 (Part 1) | Moving Picture Experts Group – 2 |
| **Standard No.** | ISO/IEC 10918, ITU-T T.81 | ISO/IEC 15444 | ISO/IEC 13818 |
| **Target** | Still images | Still images | Video + audio |
| **Year** | 1992 | 2000 | 1995 |
| **Transform** | 8×8 DCT | 5/3 or 9/7 DWT (wavelet) | 8×8 DCT |
| **Quantization** | Scalar (Q-table) | Embedded (EBCOT) | Scalar (Q-table) |
| **Entropy Coding** | Huffman (also Arithmetic) | MQ Arithmetic coder | Huffman |
| **Target Bitrate** | 0.25 – 2.5 bpp | 0.1 – 3.0 bpp | 4 – 15 Mbit/s |
| **Typical CR** | 10:1 – 20:1 | 20:1 – 50:1 | 30:1 – 100:1 |
| **Compression Type** | Lossy (lossless mode exists) | Both lossy and lossless | Lossy |
| **Key Feature** | Block-based DCT | Scalable (resolution/SNR), ROI coding | I/P/B frames, motion compensation |
| **Application** | Web images, digital cameras | Medical imaging, digital cinema | DVD, SDTV/HDTV broadcast, DVB |
| **Artifact** | 8×8 blocking | Ringing (wavelet) | Blocking + mosquito noise |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row of comparison, max 7 rows)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing MPEG-2 and H.264.** H.264 is **MPEG-4 Part 10**, not MPEG-2. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you call H.264 "MPEG-2". State the year, the ISO number, and the ITU-T number.
>
> 2. **Stating "DCT removes redundancy."** DCT does **not** remove redundancy — it merely **compacts** energy into few coefficients. **Quantization** is the step that removes redundancy (lossy). Marks deducted: 1.
>
> 3. **Confusing 4:2:0 with 4:2:2.** In **4:2:0**, Cb and Cr are subsampled by 2 horizontally **and** vertically. In **4:2:2**, only horizontal subsampling. Students routinely write "4:2:0 halves chroma in both directions" — but they then mistakenly apply it to 4:2:2. Deduct 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting DC DPCM.** JPEG does **not** Huffman-code the raw DC coefficient of every block. It codes the **difference** $DC_n - DC_{n-1}$. This is a common 1-mark loss.
>
> 5. **PSNR formula errors.** Forgetting the $20 \log_{10}$ form (or using 10 when Peak/MSE is squared) loses 1 mark. Memorize: **PSNR = $20 \log_{10}(\text{Peak}/\sqrt{\text{MSE}})$**.
>
> 6. **No units in MSE/PSNR answers.** MSE has units of "squared intensity levels"; PSNR has units of **dB**. Always write them.
>
> 7. **MPEG I-frame dependency claim.** Some students wrongly write "I-frames are used for display only and contain no data." I-frames are **fully coded** images; they are the largest single frames in the bitstream. Avoid this pitfall.
>
> 8. **Confusing "M" and "N" in GOP M/N notation.** Convention: **N = total frames in GOP**, **M = I-to-I spacing (or distance between I-frames)**. Writing them swapped will be penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — JPEG & MPEG Image Compression Standards**

### JPEG — Must-Know
- [x] JPEG is **ISO/IEC 10918** and targets **still images** only.
- [x] The pipeline is: **RGB → YCbCr → Subsample → 8×8 blocks → Level shift → DCT → Quantize → Zig-zag → DC-DPCM + AC-RLE → Huffman**.
- [x] **Quantization is the only lossy step** in baseline JPEG.
- [x] **8×8 block size** is a hard standard requirement (not configurable).
- [x] DCT is preferred over DFT because it is **real-valued** and has **better energy compaction** with no block-edge artifacts.
- [x] **4:2:0 subsampling** reduces data by 50% with no perceptual loss.
- [x] **Zig-zag scan** reorders coefficients low→high frequency to maximize RLE efficiency.
- [x] **Huffman coding** is the final lossless stage; arithmetic coding is an optional alternative.
- [x] Lossless JPEG mode exists but is rarely used; most "JPEG" is the lossy DCT mode.

### MPEG — Must-Know
- [x] MPEG = **Moving Picture Experts Group**; targets **video + audio**.
- [x] **I-frame** = intra-coded (JPEG-like), independent; anchor of GOP.
- [x] **P-frame** = forward-predicted from past I/P; stores MV + residual.
- [x] **B-frame** = bidirectional, predicted from past AND future; smallest.
- [x] **Macroblock size** = **16×16** pixels in MPEG-1/2/4.
- [x] **GOP = N/M** notation: N = total frames, M = I-to-I spacing.
- [x] **Motion vector (Δx, Δy)** stores block displacement between frames.
- [x] **Residual = current frame − motion-compensated prediction**, then DCT-compressed.
- [x] **MPEG-2** = DVD, DVB broadcast; **MPEG-4** = streaming, mobile; **H.264** = MPEG-4 Part 10, co-developed with ITU-T.
- [x] **Closed GOP** = no cross-boundary reference; **Open GOP** allows it.

### Universal Compression Metrics
- [x] **CR = $n_1/n_2$**; higher = better compression.
- [x] **R = $1 - 1/\text{CR}$**; fraction of data that was redundant.
- [x] **MSE** = mean squared error between original and reconstructed.
- [x] **PSNR = $20 \log_{10}(\text{Peak}/\sqrt{\text{MSE}})$** dB; >40 dB is excellent.
- [x] **Entropy H = $-\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$** sets the theoretical lower bound on average code length.
- [x] **Huffman efficiency** = $H / \bar{L} \times 100\%$.

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] Do not say "DCT removes redundancy" — say "DCT compacts energy; quantization removes redundancy."
- [x] Do not confuse 4:2:0 and 4:2:2 subsampling ratios.
- [x] Do not confuse MPEG-2 with H.264; H.264 is MPEG-4 Part 10.
- [x] Always state units (dB for PSNR, bits/pixel for bpp, bits/symbol for entropy).
